1:
"Let brotherly love continue." The witnesses note the charge that brotherly love be maintained. Gill notes this is not to be understood of love to all mankind, or to those of the same nation, or natural relations, but of that special love which the saints bear to one another as brethren in Christ, and which is to continue. The witnesses note Paul charging that brotherly love continue; the call to maintain the special love of the saints — that Paul, beginning his closing exhortations, presses first the grace of "brotherly love" — the warm, special affection that believers bear to one another as members of one spiritual family, sons of the same Father and brethren in Christ — and bids it "continue," that is, abide and persevere; the witnesses note that he does not bid them begin to love, for they already did, but to keep up and maintain a love already in being, against the things that would cool or break it; so that the church's first duty in its life together is the unbroken continuance of brotherly love; let brotherly love continue. The witnesses note let brotherly love. The witnesses note continue.

The witnesses note the charge that brotherly love continue — the special affection of the saints as brethren in Christ, to be maintained unbroken against all that would cool it.

So the witnesses make the verse the maintenance of brotherly love. The lesson the expositors draw is that the saints are to keep up and persevere in the special love they bear one another as brethren. The witnesses note the brotherly love: "let brotherly love" — Gill notes the special love of the saints as brethren; the brotherly love; the special love; the the saints; the brethren; the in Christ. The expositors observe the not all mankind: not love to all mankind or natural kin, but the saints' love; the not all mankind; the not natural kin; the the saints; the spiritual; the brethren. The witnesses press the continue: "continue" — let it abide and persevere; the continue; the abide; the persevere; the keep up; the maintained. The expositors draw the lesson on the already loved: he bids not begin but maintain a love in being; the already loved; the not begin; the maintain; the in being; the keep up. The witnesses observe the against cooling: against the things that would cool or break it; the against cooling; the cool; the break; the the things; the maintained. The expositors note the first duty: the church's first duty in its life together; the first duty; the the church; the its life together; the love; the unbroken. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let brotherly love, the continue, and the maintenance of love. The verse thus records the maintenance of brotherly love — teaching that the saints are to keep up and persevere in the special love they bear one another as brethren.
2:
"Be not forgetful to entertain strangers: for thereby some have entertained angels unawares." The witnesses note the duty of hospitality to strangers. Gill notes by "strangers" are meant not unconverted men, nor saints who are pilgrims, but such as are of another country and unknown; and even wicked men are not excluded. The witnesses note Paul charging that they not forget to entertain strangers, for thereby some have entertained angels unawares; the call to hospitality, with the encouragement of unawares entertaining angels — that Paul presses the grace of hospitality, especially to "strangers" — travellers and foreigners, the unknown, who in those days of persecution often needed shelter — bidding the saints "be not forgetful to entertain" them (to receive and lodge them gladly) — and he adds a sweet encouragement drawn from the past: "for thereby some have entertained angels unawares" (as Abraham and Lot, who, in welcoming travellers, found they had received angels of God, all unknowing); so that the believer is to keep an open door, knowing that hospitality may bring an unlooked-for blessing; be not forgetful to entertain strangers. The witnesses note be not forgetful to entertain strangers. The witnesses note for thereby some have entertained angels unawares.

The witnesses note the call to hospitality to strangers — to receive travellers gladly, encouraged that some, like Abraham and Lot, have entertained angels unawares.

So the witnesses make the verse the duty of hospitality. The lesson the expositors draw is that the saints are to welcome strangers gladly, knowing some have unknowingly entertained angels. The witnesses note the entertain strangers: "be not forgetful to entertain strangers" — Gill notes strangers, travellers and the unknown; the entertain strangers; the travellers; the foreigners; the the unknown; the welcome. The expositors observe the often needed shelter: in days of persecution they often needed shelter; the often needed shelter; the persecution; the shelter; the lodging; the needed. The witnesses press the receive gladly: to receive and lodge them gladly; the receive gladly; the lodge; the open door; the welcome; the hospitable. The expositors draw the lesson on the angels unawares: "for thereby some have entertained angels unawares" — as Abraham and Lot; the angels unawares; the Abraham; the Lot; the unknowing; the angels. The witnesses observe the unlooked-for blessing: hospitality may bring an unlooked-for blessing; the unlooked-for blessing; the unexpected; the the blessing; the hospitality; the reward. The expositors note the open door: the believer keeps an open door; the open door; the the believer; the keeps; the hospitable; the welcome. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the be not forgetful to entertain strangers, the for thereby some have entertained angels unawares, and the duty of hospitality. The verse thus records the duty of hospitality — teaching that the saints are to welcome strangers gladly, knowing some have unknowingly entertained angels.
3:
"Remember them that are in bonds, as bound with them; and them which suffer adversity, as being yourselves also in the body." The witnesses note the sympathy due to prisoners and the afflicted. Gill notes "them that are in bonds" are not those bound for crimes or debt, but such as are in bonds for the sake of Christ and the gospel, which was often the lot of God's people, who should be remembered. The witnesses note Paul charging that they remember those in bonds as bound with them, and those who suffer adversity as being themselves in the body; the fellow-feeling that enters into the sufferings of others as one's own — that Paul calls for a tender sympathy with the persecuted: "remember them that are in bonds" (the prisoners suffering for Christ's sake), and that "as bound with them" (entering into their case as if sharing their very chains, by prayer, comfort, and aid) — and "them which suffer adversity," remembering them "as being yourselves also in the body" (as men still in the body, liable to the same sufferings, who may one day need the like compassion); so that Christian fellow-feeling is to put itself in the place of the afflicted, sympathizing as if it were our own suffering; remember them that are in bonds, as bound with them. The witnesses note remember them that are in bonds, as bound with them. The witnesses note them which suffer adversity, as being yourselves also in the body.

The witnesses note the sympathy due to the persecuted — remembering prisoners as bound with them, and the afflicted as being ourselves in the body, liable to the same sufferings.

So the witnesses make the verse the fellow-feeling with the suffering. The lesson the expositors draw is that the saints are to enter into the sufferings of prisoners and the afflicted as if their own. The witnesses note the in bonds: "remember them that are in bonds" — Gill notes prisoners for Christ's sake; the in bonds; the prisoners; the for Christ; the the gospel; the suffering. The expositors observe the as bound with them: "as bound with them" — as if sharing their chains; the as bound with them; the sharing; the their chains; the by prayer; the aid. The witnesses press the suffer adversity: "and them which suffer adversity" — the afflicted; the suffer adversity; the the afflicted; the the suffering; the troubled; the distressed. The expositors draw the lesson on the in the body: "as being yourselves also in the body" — as men liable to the same; the in the body; the liable; the the same sufferings; the may need; the compassion. The witnesses observe the put in their place: fellow-feeling puts itself in the place of the afflicted; the put in their place; the fellow-feeling; the the afflicted; the sympathize; the their case. The expositors note the as our own: sympathizing as if it were our own suffering; the as our own; the our own suffering; the sympathize; the enter into; the fellow-feeling. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the remember them that are in bonds as bound with them, the them which suffer adversity as being yourselves also in the body, and the fellow-feeling. The verse thus records the fellow-feeling with the suffering — teaching that the saints are to enter into the sufferings of prisoners and the afflicted as if their own.
4:
"Marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled: but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge." The witnesses note the honour of marriage and the judgment on the unchaste. Gill notes "marriage" is the union of one man and one woman in wedlock, whereby they become one flesh; some read it as an exhortation, "let it be honourable," others as an assertion, it is honourable. The witnesses note Paul declaring that marriage is honourable in all and the bed undefiled, but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge; the honour of marriage upheld and the impure threatened with judgment — that Paul sets the dignity of marriage against all sexual sin: "marriage is honourable in all" (a holy and honourable estate, open to all sorts of men, not to be despised as some falsely taught) "and the bed undefiled" (the marriage bed is pure and chaste, no defilement in the lawful use of it) — "but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge" (those who defile themselves with fornication or break the marriage bond by adultery shall fall under God's righteous judgment); so that Paul honours the marriage God ordained and warns solemnly against the impurity that profanes it; marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled. The witnesses note marriage is honourable in all, and the bed undefiled. The witnesses note but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge.

The witnesses note marriage upheld as honourable and the bed undefiled — a holy estate open to all — while whoremongers and adulterers are threatened with God's righteous judgment.

So the witnesses make the verse the honour of marriage and judgment on the impure. The lesson the expositors draw is that marriage is a holy, honourable estate, while sexual sin falls under God's judgment. The witnesses note the honourable: "marriage is honourable in all" — Gill notes a holy estate, open to all; the honourable; the a holy estate; the open to all; the not despised; the dignity. The expositors observe the the bed undefiled: "and the bed undefiled" — the marriage bed is pure and chaste; the the bed undefiled; the pure; the chaste; the lawful use; the no defilement. The witnesses press the whoremongers: "but whoremongers and adulterers" — those who defile by fornication or adultery; the whoremongers; the adulterers; the fornication; the adultery; the impure. The expositors draw the lesson on the God will judge: "God will judge" — they fall under God's righteous judgment; the God will judge; the righteous judgment; the punished; the condemned; the the impure. The witnesses observe the honours marriage: Paul honours the marriage God ordained; the honours marriage; the God ordained; the the estate; the holy; the upheld. The expositors note the warns against impurity: and warns solemnly against the impurity that profanes it; the warns against impurity; the the impurity; the profanes; the the warning; the judged. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the marriage is honourable in all and the bed undefiled, the but whoremongers and adulterers God will judge, and the honour of marriage. The verse thus records the honour of marriage and judgment on the impure — teaching that marriage is a holy, honourable estate, while sexual sin falls under God's judgment.
5:
"Let your conversation be without covetousness; and be content with such things as ye have: for he hath said, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee." The witnesses note the charge to contentment, grounded in God's promised presence. Gill notes covetousness is an immoderate desire of riches, an over-anxious care for worldly things, attended with dissatisfaction and discontent with one's present state. The witnesses note Paul charging that their manner of life be without covetousness, content with what they have, since God has said he will never leave nor forsake them; the contentment that rests on God's unfailing presence — that Paul bids the believers' whole "conversation" (manner of life) be "without covetousness" (free from the greedy, anxious craving for riches) and instead marked by contentment, "content with such things as ye have" — and he gives the firmest ground for such contentment: God's own promise, "for he hath said, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee"; the witnesses note the strength of this assurance — since God himself has pledged never to abandon his own, the believer who has God needs nothing else, and may rest content in any earthly lot, his portion being the unfailing presence of God; for he hath said, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee. The witnesses note let your conversation be without covetousness; and be content with such things as ye have. The witnesses note for he hath said, I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee.

The witnesses note the charge to contentment, free of covetousness — grounded in God's own promise never to leave nor forsake; he who has God needs nothing else, content in any lot.

So the witnesses make the verse the contentment grounded in God's presence. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to be free of covetousness and content, resting on God's promise never to forsake him. The witnesses note the without covetousness: "let your conversation be without covetousness" — Gill notes free from the greedy craving for riches; the without covetousness; the greedy craving; the anxious; the riches; the free. The expositors observe the content: "be content with such things as ye have" — content with what they have; the content; the with what they have; the satisfied; the their lot; the resting. The witnesses press the he hath said: "for he hath said" — God's own promise the ground; the he hath said; the God's promise; the the ground; the pledged; the firm. The expositors draw the lesson on the never leave: "I will never leave thee, nor forsake thee" — God will never abandon his own; the never leave; the nor forsake; the never abandon; the his own; the pledged. The witnesses observe the has God needs nothing: he who has God needs nothing else; the has God needs nothing; the has God; the nothing else; the content; the the portion. The expositors note the any lot: content in any earthly lot, his portion being God's presence; the any lot; the earthly; the his portion; the God's presence; the content. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let your conversation be without covetousness and be content with such things as ye have, the for he hath said I will never leave thee nor forsake thee, and the contentment grounded in God. The verse thus records the contentment grounded in God's presence — teaching that the believer is to be free of covetousness and content, resting on God's promise never to forsake him.
6:
"So that we may boldly say, The Lord is my helper, and I will not fear what man shall do unto me." The witnesses note the bold confidence that springs from God's promise. Barnes notes that without any hesitation or doubt, in all times of perplexity and threatening want, when we scarcely know whence supplies are to come, we may put our trust in God. The witnesses note Paul declaring that, so assured, we may boldly say, the Lord is my helper, and I will not fear what man shall do; the holy boldness of faith resting on God's help — that Paul draws the triumphant conclusion from God's promise (v.5): "so that we may boldly say" (with full confidence and no doubt) "The Lord is my helper, and I will not fear what man shall do unto me"; the witnesses note the reasoning of faith — since God has pledged never to forsake us, and is himself our helper, we are lifted above all fear of man, for what can man do to one whom the Almighty helps? so that the believer, leaning on God's promise, can face every threat and every enemy with a holy fearlessness, knowing his Helper is greater than all that man can do; The Lord is my helper, and I will not fear what man shall do unto me. The witnesses note so that we may boldly say, The Lord is my helper. The witnesses note I will not fear what man shall do unto me.

The witnesses note the holy boldness springing from God's promise — "the Lord is my helper, I will not fear what man shall do"; faith lifted above all fear of man by the Almighty's help.

So the witnesses make the verse the bold confidence resting on God's help. The lesson the expositors draw is that, since God is our helper, the believer is lifted above all fear of what man can do. The witnesses note the boldly say: "so that we may boldly say" — Barnes notes without hesitation or doubt; the boldly say; the no doubt; the no hesitation; the confidence; the assured. The expositors observe the the Lord is my helper: "The Lord is my helper" — God himself our helper; the the Lord is my helper; the God; the our helper; the helps; the himself. The witnesses press the not fear man: "and I will not fear what man shall do unto me" — lifted above all fear of man; the not fear man; the above fear; the of man; the fearless; the bold. The expositors draw the lesson on the what can man do: what can man do to one whom God helps; the what can man do; the to one; the God helps; the fearless; the greater. The witnesses observe the reasoning of faith: faith reasons from God's pledge to fearlessness; the reasoning of faith; the God's pledge; the fearlessness; the leans; the confident. The expositors note the every threat: the believer faces every threat and enemy with holy fearlessness; the every threat; the every enemy; the holy fearlessness; the faces; the bold. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the so that we may boldly say The Lord is my helper, the I will not fear what man shall do unto me, and the bold confidence. The verse thus records the bold confidence resting on God's help — teaching that, since God is our helper, the believer is lifted above all fear of what man can do.
7:
"Remember them which have the rule over you, who have spoken unto you the word of God: whose faith follow, considering the end of their conversation." The witnesses note the duty to remember and imitate faithful spiritual rulers. Gill notes Christ's church is a kingdom, and he is King in it; pastors of churches are subordinate governors, who rule well when they rule not arbitrarily by their own wills, but according to the laws of Christ, with faithfulness, prudence, and diligence. The witnesses note Paul charging that they remember their rulers who have spoken the word of God, follow their faith, and consider the end of their life; the honour due to faithful leaders, to be remembered and imitated — that Paul bids the believers honour their spiritual guides: "remember them which have the rule over you" (the pastors and teachers set over them, perhaps now departed) "who have spoken unto you the word of God" (who faithfully preached God's truth to them) — and twofold: "whose faith follow" (imitate their believing, their steadfast trust in God) "considering the end of their conversation" (marking how their manner of life ended, the blessed issue of their faithful course, perhaps in a holy death or martyrdom); so that the saints are to keep their faithful leaders in mind and to copy the faith that carried them through to a good end; whose faith follow, considering the end of their conversation. The witnesses note remember them which have the rule over you, who have spoken unto you the word of God. The witnesses note whose faith follow, considering the end of their conversation.

The witnesses note the honour due to faithful leaders — to remember those who spoke God's word, follow their faith, and consider the blessed end of their faithful course.

So the witnesses make the verse the remembering and imitating of faithful leaders. The lesson the expositors draw is that the saints are to honour their guides, follow their faith, and mark the good end of their lives. The witnesses note the remember: "remember them which have the rule over you" — Gill notes the pastors set over them; the remember; the the rule over you; the pastors; the guides; the set over. The expositors observe the spoken the word: "who have spoken unto you the word of God" — who faithfully preached God's truth; the spoken the word; the preached; the God's truth; the faithfully; the the word. The witnesses press the follow their faith: "whose faith follow" — imitate their steadfast faith; the follow their faith; the imitate; the steadfast; the their trust; the copy. The expositors draw the lesson on the end of their conversation: "considering the end of their conversation" — marking how their life ended; the end of their conversation; the their life; the ended; the the issue; the death. The witnesses observe the blessed issue: the blessed issue of their faithful course, perhaps in martyrdom; the blessed issue; the their course; the faithful; the martyrdom; the holy death. The expositors note the copy the faith: to copy the faith that carried them through; the copy the faith; the carried them; the through; the to a good end; the imitate. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the remember them which have the rule over you who have spoken unto you the word of God, the whose faith follow considering the end of their conversation, and the honour due to leaders. The verse thus records the remembering and imitating of faithful leaders — teaching that the saints are to honour their guides, follow their faith, and mark the good end of their lives.
8:
"Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and to day, and for ever." The witnesses note the unchangeableness of Christ. Gill notes Christ is the substance of the word spoken by the rulers mentioned, the author and object of their faith, and the end in which their conversation terminated; and these words express the duration and immutability of Christ. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Jesus Christ is the same yesterday, today, and for ever; the eternal sameness and immutability of Christ as the anchor of faith — that Paul, having bidden them follow the faith of their departed leaders, points them to the unchanging object of that faith: "Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and to day, and for ever" — the leaders pass away, but Christ, in whom they trusted, abides forever the same; the witnesses note the fulness of this — Christ is the same in his person, his love, his power, his promises, and his grace, through all time: "yesterday" (in ages past, the same Saviour the old saints trusted), "today" (the same now to the present church), "and for ever" (unchangeably the same to all eternity); so that the believer rests on an immutable Christ, who never changes though all else does; Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and to day, and for ever. The witnesses note Jesus Christ the same yesterday, and to day. The witnesses note and for ever.

The witnesses note the unchangeableness of Christ — the same yesterday, today, and for ever; the leaders pass, but Christ, the object of their faith, abides forever the same.

So the witnesses make the verse the eternal sameness of Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, the object of faith, is unchangeably the same through all time, the anchor of the believer. The witnesses note the same: "Jesus Christ the same" — Gill notes Christ the unchanging object of faith; the the same; the unchanging; the the object; the of faith; the immutable. The expositors observe the leaders pass: the leaders pass away, but Christ abides; the leaders pass; the pass away; the Christ abides; the forever; the the same. The witnesses press the yesterday: "yesterday" — the same Saviour the old saints trusted; the yesterday; the ages past; the the old saints; the trusted; the the same. The expositors draw the lesson on the today: "and to day" — the same now to the present church; the today; the now; the the present church; the the same; the present. The witnesses observe the for ever: "and for ever" — unchangeably the same to all eternity; the for ever; the all eternity; the unchangeable; the the same; the abides. The expositors note the in all things: the same in person, love, power, promises, grace; the in all things; the his person; the his love; the his power; the his grace. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Jesus Christ the same yesterday and to day, the and for ever, and the unchangeableness of Christ. The verse thus records the eternal sameness of Christ — teaching that Christ, the object of faith, is unchangeably the same through all time, the anchor of the believer.
9:
"Be not carried about with divers and strange doctrines. For it is a good thing that the heart be established with grace; not with meats, which have not profited them that have been occupied therein." The witnesses note the warning against strange doctrines and the value of a heart established with grace. Gill notes the word "divers" denotes the variety and multitude of other doctrines, referring to the rites of the law, the traditions of the elders, or the doctrines of men, whether Jews or Gentiles. The witnesses note Paul charging that they not be carried about with divers and strange doctrines, but have the heart established with grace, not with meats; the steadfastness in grace against the unstable winds of strange teaching — that Paul, having set the unchanging Christ before them, warns against doctrinal instability: "be not carried about with divers and strange doctrines" (do not be tossed and swept along by the many novel and alien teachings, especially the Jewish ceremonial notions about foods) — for "it is a good thing that the heart be established with grace" (the heart is made firm and stable by the grace of God and the doctrine of grace), "not with meats, which have not profited them that have been occupied therein"; the witnesses note the contrast — the old observances about meats and ceremonies never profited those who trusted in them, while grace truly establishes the soul; so that the heart is to be grounded on grace, not on outward ceremonies; be not carried about with divers and strange doctrines. The witnesses note be not carried about with divers and strange doctrines. The witnesses note it is a good thing that the heart be established with grace; not with meats.

The witnesses note the warning against strange doctrines — the heart established with grace, not with meats; grace truly grounds the soul, while ceremonies never profited those who trusted them.

So the witnesses make the verse the steadfastness in grace against strange teaching. The lesson the expositors draw is that the heart is established by grace, not by outward ceremonies, which never profited. The witnesses note the not carried about: "be not carried about with divers and strange doctrines" — Gill notes not tossed by novel, alien teachings; the not carried about; the divers; the strange doctrines; the novel; the alien. The expositors observe the the ceremonial notions: especially the Jewish ceremonial notions about foods; the the ceremonial notions; the Jewish; the foods; the the law; the the traditions. The witnesses press the established with grace: "it is a good thing that the heart be established with grace" — the heart made firm by grace; the established with grace; the the heart; the firm; the stable; the grace. The expositors draw the lesson on the not with meats: "not with meats" — not by foods and ceremonies; the not with meats; the foods; the ceremonies; the outward; the not. The witnesses observe the not profited: "which have not profited them that have been occupied therein" — meats never profited those who trusted them; the not profited; the never profited; the trusted them; the the observances; the empty. The expositors note the grounded on grace: the heart is to be grounded on grace, not ceremony; the grounded on grace; the grace; the not ceremony; the the soul; the established. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the be not carried about with divers and strange doctrines, the it is a good thing that the heart be established with grace not with meats, and the steadfastness in grace. The verse thus records the steadfastness in grace against strange teaching — teaching that the heart is established by grace, not by outward ceremonies.
10:
"We have an altar, whereof they have no right to eat which serve the tabernacle." The witnesses note the believer's altar in Christ, of which the legal servants have no part. Gill notes by "altar" is meant not the cross, nor the Lord's table, but Christ himself, who is altar, sacrifice, and priest, typified by the altar of burnt offering. The witnesses note Paul declaring that we have an altar, of which they have no right to eat who serve the tabernacle; the Christian's altar in Christ surpassing the legal sacrifices — that Paul sets the believer's spiritual portion against the Jewish ritual: "we have an altar" — and the witnesses note Gill takes this altar to be Christ himself (altar, sacrifice, and priest in one), not a literal table or the cross only — "whereof they have no right to eat which serve the tabernacle" (those who still cling to the legal worship and its sacrifices have no part in this); the witnesses note the meaning — those who rest in the old tabernacle service and refuse Christ have no right to feed by faith on him, the true altar and sacrifice; so that the believer's true and abiding altar is Christ, in whom the gospel has a better feast than the law ever gave; we have an altar, whereof they have no right to eat which serve the tabernacle. The witnesses note we have an altar. The witnesses note whereof they have no right to eat which serve the tabernacle.

The witnesses note the believer's altar in Christ — taken by Gill as Christ himself, altar, sacrifice, and priest — of which those who cling to the legal tabernacle have no part.

So the witnesses make the verse the Christian's altar in Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that believers have an altar in Christ, of which those who cling to the old tabernacle service have no share. The witnesses note the an altar: "we have an altar" — Gill notes the altar is Christ himself; the an altar; the Christ himself; the altar; the sacrifice; the priest. The expositors present the reading: the witnesses note Gill takes the altar to be Christ (altar, sacrifice, and priest in one), not a literal table or the cross only. The witnesses press the no right to eat: "whereof they have no right to eat" — those of the tabernacle have no part; the no right to eat; the no part; the the legal; the the tabernacle; the excluded. The expositors draw the lesson on the serve the tabernacle: "which serve the tabernacle" — who cling to the legal worship; the serve the tabernacle; the cling; the the legal worship; the the sacrifices; the the old. The witnesses observe the refuse Christ: those who rest in the old and refuse Christ have no part; the refuse Christ; the rest in the old; the no part; the feed by faith; the excluded. The expositors note the better feast: the gospel has a better feast than the law gave; the better feast; the the gospel; the than the law; the Christ; the the altar. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we have an altar, the whereof they have no right to eat which serve the tabernacle, and the altar in Christ. The verse thus records the Christian's altar in Christ — teaching that believers have an altar in Christ, of which those who cling to the old tabernacle service have no share.
11:
"For the bodies of those beasts, whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin, are burned without the camp." The witnesses note the type of the sin-offering burned outside the camp. Gill notes this refers not to the red heifer nor the sin offering in general, but to the bullock and goat on the day of atonement, whose blood was brought into the most holy place by the high priest, and whose bodies were burned without the camp. The witnesses note Paul observing that the bodies of the beasts whose blood was brought into the sanctuary for sin were burned outside the camp; the legal type pointing to Christ's suffering outside the gate — that Paul draws out a striking detail of the Day of Atonement ritual: "the bodies of those beasts, whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin" (the bullock and the goat, whose blood the high priest carried into the most holy place to make atonement) "are burned without the camp"; the witnesses note this as a deliberate type — the very animals whose blood made atonement had their bodies carried outside the camp and there consumed; so that the law itself foreshadowed that the great Sin-bearer, whose blood would make the true atonement, must suffer outside the camp, away from the holy city, as the next verse declares of Christ; the bodies of those beasts... are burned without the camp. The witnesses note whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin. The witnesses note are burned without the camp.

The witnesses note the type of the sin-offering — the beasts whose blood made atonement burned outside the camp; the law foreshadowing that the true Sin-bearer must suffer outside the gate.

So the witnesses make the verse the legal type pointing to Christ's suffering outside the camp. The lesson the expositors draw is that the sin-offering burned outside the camp foreshadowed Christ suffering outside the gate. The witnesses note the those beasts: "the bodies of those beasts" — Gill notes the bullock and goat of the Day of Atonement; the those beasts; the the bullock; the the goat; the the Day of Atonement; the the sin-offering. The expositors observe the blood for sin: "whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin" — the blood carried into the most holy place; the blood for sin; the the sanctuary; the the high priest; the atonement; the the blood. The witnesses press the burned without the camp: "are burned without the camp" — the bodies consumed outside the camp; the burned without the camp; the the bodies; the consumed; the outside; the the camp. The expositors draw the lesson on the deliberate type: this was a deliberate type; the deliberate type; the the type; the foreshadowed; the the law; the figure. The witnesses observe the the Sin-bearer: foreshadowing that the true Sin-bearer must suffer outside; the the Sin-bearer; the the true atonement; the must suffer; the outside; the Christ. The expositors note the away from the city: away from the holy city, as Christ did; the away from the city; the the holy city; the Christ; the outside; the suffered. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whose blood is brought into the sanctuary by the high priest for sin, the are burned without the camp, and the legal type. The verse thus records the legal type pointing to Christ's suffering outside the camp — teaching that the sin-offering burned outside foreshadowed Christ suffering outside the gate.
12:
"Wherefore Jesus also, that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate." The witnesses note that Christ, to fulfil the type, suffered outside the gate. Barnes notes that there might be a conformity between his death for sin and the sacrifices which typified it; and it is implied here that it was voluntary on the part of Jesus that he suffered. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Jesus, to sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered outside the gate; the fulfilment of the type in Christ's suffering outside the city — that Paul applies the type to Christ: "wherefore Jesus also" — to answer the figure of the sin-offering burned outside the camp — "that he might sanctify the people with his own blood" (to set them apart and cleanse them by his atoning blood, not the blood of beasts) "suffered without the gate"; the witnesses note the exact correspondence — as the bodies of the atonement-sacrifices were burned outside the camp, so Christ, the true sacrifice, was led out and crucified outside the gate of Jerusalem; so that the very place of his suffering fulfilled the ancient type, and his own blood, shed there, accomplished the sanctification of his people which the beasts' blood only foreshadowed; that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, suffered without the gate. The witnesses note that he might sanctify the people with his own blood. The witnesses note suffered without the gate.

The witnesses note Christ fulfilling the type — suffering outside the gate to sanctify his people with his own blood; the place of his death answering the sin-offering burned outside the camp.

So the witnesses make the verse the fulfilment of the type in Christ's suffering. The lesson the expositors draw is that Christ, the true sacrifice, suffered outside the gate to sanctify his people with his own blood. The witnesses note the Jesus also: "wherefore Jesus also" — Barnes notes to conform to the typifying sacrifices; the Jesus also; the to answer the type; the conform; the the figure; the the sacrifices. The expositors observe the sanctify: "that he might sanctify the people with his own blood" — to cleanse them by his atoning blood; the sanctify; the the people; the his own blood; the cleanse; the atoning. The witnesses press the not beasts' blood: not the blood of beasts but his own; the not beasts' blood; the his own; the atoning; the cleanse; the the people. The expositors draw the lesson on the suffered without the gate: "suffered without the gate" — crucified outside Jerusalem's gate; the suffered without the gate; the crucified; the outside; the Jerusalem; the the gate. The witnesses observe the exact correspondence: as the sacrifices were burned outside, so Christ suffered outside; the exact correspondence; the the sacrifices; the burned outside; the Christ; the suffered outside. The expositors note the fulfilled the type: the place of his suffering fulfilled the ancient type; the fulfilled the type; the the place; the the ancient type; the Christ; the answered. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that he might sanctify the people with his own blood, the suffered without the gate, and the fulfilment of the type. The verse thus records the fulfilment of the type in Christ's suffering — teaching that Christ, the true sacrifice, suffered outside the gate to sanctify his people with his own blood.
13:
"Let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp, bearing his reproach." The witnesses note the call to go forth to Christ outside the camp, bearing his reproach. Gill notes "let us go forth unto him without the camp" — either of legal ceremonies, which are to be quitted, or of the world, compared to a camp for its instability and hostility, full of enemies to Christ and his people. The witnesses note Paul charging that we go forth to Christ outside the camp, bearing his reproach; the call to leave the old order and the world to share Christ's reproach — that Paul draws the practical lesson from Christ's suffering outside the gate: "let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp" — let us leave behind the "camp" (whether the abandoned system of Jewish ceremonies, or the hostile world itself) and go out to Christ where he is, outside the gate — "bearing his reproach"; the witnesses note the cost and the glory of this — to follow Christ outside the camp is to share the shame and reproach that fell on him, to be willing to be cast out and despised by the world for his sake, as he was; so that the believer is called to a decisive separation, leaving the old and the worldly to take his place with the rejected Christ, gladly bearing the reproach that is his honour; let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp, bearing his reproach. The witnesses note let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp. The witnesses note bearing his reproach.

The witnesses note the call to go forth to Christ outside the camp — leaving the old order and the world to share his reproach; a decisive separation to take one's place with the rejected Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to share Christ's reproach outside the camp. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to leave the old and the worldly to go out to Christ, gladly bearing his reproach. The witnesses note the go forth: "let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp" — Gill notes leave the camp of ceremonies or the world; the go forth; the without the camp; the the ceremonies; the the world; the leave. The expositors observe the the camp: the "camp" is the abandoned Jewish system or the hostile world; the the camp; the the Jewish system; the the world; the hostile; the abandoned. The witnesses press the unto him: go out to Christ where he is, outside the gate; the unto him; the to Christ; the where he is; the outside; the the gate. The expositors draw the lesson on the bearing his reproach: "bearing his reproach" — sharing the shame that fell on him; the bearing his reproach; the the shame; the fell on him; the share; the despised. The witnesses observe the cast out: to be willing to be cast out and despised for his sake; the cast out; the despised; the for his sake; the the world; the willing. The expositors note the decisive separation: a decisive separation, taking one's place with the rejected Christ; the decisive separation; the leaving the old; the the rejected Christ; the take his place; the gladly. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let us go forth therefore unto him without the camp, the bearing his reproach, and the sharing his reproach. The verse thus records the call to share Christ's reproach outside the camp — teaching that the believer is to leave the old and the worldly to go out to Christ, gladly bearing his reproach.
14:
"For here have we no continuing city, but we seek one to come." The witnesses note that the believer has no abiding city here, but seeks the city to come. Gill notes "here have we no continuing city" — neither for religious worship (Jerusalem soon to be destroyed) nor for civil life; this had a peculiar regard to the Hebrews, whose temple and city would shortly be desolate. The witnesses note Paul declaring that here we have no continuing city, but we seek one to come; the pilgrim outlook that loosens the heart from earth and fixes it on the city to come — that Paul gives the reason for going out to Christ and bearing reproach (v.13): the believer has nothing abiding to lose here — "for here have we no continuing city" (no permanent home or settled place on earth, least of all the doomed earthly Jerusalem) "but we seek one to come"; the witnesses note the pilgrim spirit this breeds — since there is no lasting city below, the heart is set free to seek the one that is to come, the heavenly and abiding city of God; so that the loss of earthly things is no real loss to the pilgrim whose true home is yet ahead; for here have we no continuing city, but we seek one to come. The witnesses note for here have we no continuing city. The witnesses note but we seek one to come.

The witnesses note the pilgrim outlook — here we have no continuing city, but seek one to come; the heart loosed from earth and fixed on the heavenly, abiding city of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the pilgrim outlook seeking the city to come. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer, having no abiding city here, sets his heart on the heavenly city to come. The witnesses note the no continuing city: "here have we no continuing city" — Gill notes no permanent home on earth; the no continuing city; the no permanent home; the on earth; the the doomed Jerusalem; the no settled place. The expositors observe the the Hebrews: with peculiar regard to the Hebrews, whose city would be desolate; the the Hebrews; the their city; the desolate; the soon; the destroyed. The witnesses press the seek one to come: "but we seek one to come" — we seek the city to come; the seek one to come; the the city to come; the heavenly; the abiding; the sought. The expositors draw the lesson on the pilgrim spirit: this breeds the pilgrim spirit; the pilgrim spirit; the bred; the loosed; the the heart; the set free. The witnesses observe the heart set free: the heart is set free to seek the abiding city; the heart set free; the loosed; the the abiding city; the of God; the seek. The expositors note the no real loss: the loss of earthly things is no real loss to the pilgrim; the no real loss; the earthly things; the the pilgrim; the his home ahead; the not lost. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for here have we no continuing city, the but we seek one to come, and the pilgrim outlook. The verse thus records the pilgrim outlook seeking the city to come — teaching that the believer, having no abiding city here, sets his heart on the heavenly city to come.
15:
"By him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually, that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name." The witnesses note the gospel sacrifice of praise offered through Christ. Gill notes this sacrifice of praise is for temporal and spiritual mercies, particularly for expiation of sin by the blood of Christ and for the continuing city to come; it is not propitiatory, but spiritual and evangelical. The witnesses note Paul charging that by Christ we offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually, the fruit of our lips giving thanks; the spiritual sacrifice of thanksgiving that the gospel believer offers through Christ — that Paul, in place of the abolished animal sacrifices, names the sacrifices that remain under the gospel, beginning with praise: "by him therefore" (through Christ, the only altar and mediator, by whom alone our worship is accepted) "let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually" — a constant, unceasing offering of thanksgiving — "that is, the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name"; the witnesses note that this is the Christian's true sacrifice — not the blood of beasts, but the heartfelt praise and thanksgiving of redeemed lips, offered through Christ to God; so that the believer's continual worship is to bless and thank God for all his mercies, above all for salvation in Christ; let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually. The witnesses note by him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually. The witnesses note the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name.

The witnesses note the gospel sacrifice of praise — offered through Christ continually, the fruit of our lips giving thanks; the believer's true sacrifice the heartfelt praise of redeemed lips.

So the witnesses make the verse the spiritual sacrifice of praise through Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the gospel believer's sacrifice is the continual praise and thanksgiving offered to God through Christ. The witnesses note the by him: "by him therefore" — Gill notes through Christ, the only mediator; the by him; the through Christ; the the mediator; the the altar; the accepted. The expositors observe the sacrifice of praise: "let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God" — the offering of praise; the sacrifice of praise; the the offering; the praise; the to God; the worship. The witnesses press the continually: "continually" — a constant, unceasing offering; the continually; the constant; the unceasing; the always; the offered. The expositors draw the lesson on the fruit of lips: "the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name" — the thanksgiving of redeemed lips; the fruit of lips; the giving thanks; the his name; the the lips; the thanksgiving. The witnesses observe the not beasts: not the blood of beasts but heartfelt praise; the not beasts; the not blood; the heartfelt praise; the the lips; the spiritual. The expositors note the for all mercies: to bless God for all his mercies, above all salvation; the for all mercies; the bless God; the salvation; the in Christ; the thanks. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the by him therefore let us offer the sacrifice of praise to God continually, the the fruit of our lips giving thanks to his name, and the sacrifice of praise. The verse thus records the spiritual sacrifice of praise through Christ — teaching that the gospel believer's sacrifice is the continual praise and thanksgiving offered to God through Christ.
16:
"But to do good and to communicate forget not: for with such sacrifices God is well pleased." The witnesses note the sacrifices of beneficence that please God. Gill notes this is to be understood not of doing good works in general, but of acts of beneficence, or communicating to the poor; the apostle proceeds to another sort of sacrifice continuing under the gospel. The witnesses note Paul charging that they forget not to do good and to communicate, for with such sacrifices God is well pleased; the sacrifice of charity and generosity well-pleasing to God — that Paul names a second gospel sacrifice alongside praise: "but to do good and to communicate forget not" — let them not neglect deeds of kindness ("to do good") and the sharing of their goods with the needy ("to communicate," giving to the poor) — and he marks God's delight in such offerings: "for with such sacrifices God is well pleased"; the witnesses note that under the gospel the acceptable sacrifices are not animals but praise to God and goodness to men, and God is as truly pleased with deeds of charity as with worship; so that the believer's whole life of thankful praise and generous love is a continual, God-pleasing sacrifice; for with such sacrifices God is well pleased. The witnesses note but to do good and to communicate forget not. The witnesses note for with such sacrifices God is well pleased.

The witnesses note the sacrifices of beneficence — doing good and communicating to the needy — with which God is well pleased; charity to men as truly a sacrifice as praise to God.

So the witnesses make the verse the sacrifice of charity that pleases God. The lesson the expositors draw is that doing good and sharing with the needy are gospel sacrifices well-pleasing to God. The witnesses note the do good: "but to do good... forget not" — Gill notes deeds of kindness; the do good; the deeds of kindness; the beneficence; the not neglect; the good. The expositors observe the communicate: "and to communicate" — sharing goods with the needy; the communicate; the sharing; the the needy; the the poor; the give. The witnesses press the forget not: "forget not" — let them not neglect these; the forget not; the not neglect; the these; the the duty; the remember. The expositors draw the lesson on the well pleased: "for with such sacrifices God is well pleased" — God delights in such offerings; the well pleased; the God delights; the such sacrifices; the the offerings; the pleased. The witnesses observe the charity a sacrifice: charity to men is as truly a sacrifice as praise; the charity a sacrifice; the to men; the as praise; the truly; the sacrifice. The expositors note the whole life: the believer's whole life of praise and love is a sacrifice; the whole life; the praise and love; the a sacrifice; the continual; the pleasing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but to do good and to communicate forget not, the for with such sacrifices God is well pleased, and the sacrifice of charity. The verse thus records the sacrifice of charity that pleases God — teaching that doing good and sharing with the needy are gospel sacrifices well-pleasing to God.
17:
"Obey them that have the rule over you, and submit yourselves: for they watch for your souls, as they that must give account, that they may do it with joy, and not with grief: for that is unprofitable for you." The witnesses note the duty of obedience and submission to faithful spiritual rulers. Chrysostom notes anarchy is an evil and the occasion of many calamities, the source of disorder and confusion; as a chorus without its leader or an army without its commander falls into disorder, so it is with a flock without its shepherd. The witnesses note Paul charging that they obey and submit to their rulers, who watch for their souls as those that must give account; the obedience due to pastors who watch for souls and will give account — that Paul presses the duty toward faithful spiritual guides: "obey them that have the rule over you, and submit yourselves" (yield a dutiful obedience and submission to their godly leadership) — and he gives the weighty reason: "for they watch for your souls" (the pastors keep a sleepless guard over the souls of their flock, caring for their eternal welfare) "as they that must give account" (knowing they must one day answer to God for those souls) — "that they may do it with joy, and not with grief: for that is unprofitable for you"; the witnesses note that the people's obedience makes the pastors' watch a joy, while their disobedience makes it a grief, and the loss of such grief falls on the people themselves; obey them that have the rule over you, and submit yourselves. The witnesses note obey them that have the rule over you, and submit yourselves: for they watch for your souls. The witnesses note that they may do it with joy, and not with grief.

The witnesses note the obedience due to faithful pastors — who watch for souls as those that must give account; the people's obedience making the watch a joy, their disobedience a grief.

So the witnesses make the verse the obedience due to faithful pastors. The lesson the expositors draw is that the people are to obey and submit to their guides, who watch for souls and must give account. The witnesses note the obey: "obey them that have the rule over you, and submit yourselves" — yield dutiful obedience to godly leaders; the obey; the submit; the the rulers; the dutiful; the leadership. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes anarchy is an evil, a source of confusion; the anarchy; the an evil; the confusion; the the leader; the order. The witnesses press the watch for souls: "for they watch for your souls" — the pastors keep sleepless guard over souls; the watch for souls; the sleepless guard; the the souls; the the flock; the eternal welfare. The expositors draw the lesson on the give account: "as they that must give account" — they must answer to God for those souls; the give account; the answer to God; the for souls; the the reckoning; the must. The witnesses observe the with joy: "that they may do it with joy, and not with grief" — obedience makes the watch a joy; the with joy; the obedience; the a joy. The expositors note the unprofitable for you: "for that is unprofitable for you" — the loss of their grief falls on the people; the unprofitable for you; the the loss; the their own. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the obey them that have the rule over you, the that they may do it with joy and not with grief, and the obedience due to pastors. The verse thus records the obedience due to faithful pastors — teaching that the people are to obey and submit to their guides, who watch for souls and must give account.
18:
"Pray for us: for we trust we have a good conscience, in all things willing to live honestly." The witnesses note the apostle's request for the prayers of the believers. Gill notes "pray for us" — for those in the ministry, their guides and governors, since the work is of such moment, arduous, and awful, and they have such a concern in it. The witnesses note Paul asking the believers to pray for him and his fellow-ministers, since they trust they have a good conscience, willing to live honestly; the request for intercession, grounded in the integrity of his conscience — that Paul, having charged the people's duty to their leaders, asks in turn for their prayers: "pray for us" (for him and his fellow-labourers in the gospel) — and he commends himself to their confidence by his integrity: "for we trust we have a good conscience" (a conscience clear of guile and hypocrisy before God and men), "in all things willing to live honestly" (sincerely desiring to walk uprightly in everything); the witnesses note that Paul, conscious of his own honest and upright aim, the more freely asks their prayers, since they could pray for one whose conscience and conduct they could trust; pray for us: for we trust we have a good conscience. The witnesses note pray for us. The witnesses note for we trust we have a good conscience, in all things willing to live honestly.

The witnesses note Paul's request for the believers' prayers — grounded in the integrity of his conscience; a conscience clear of guile, willing in all things to live honestly.

So the witnesses make the verse the request for prayer grounded in a good conscience. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul asks the believers' prayers, commending himself by the integrity of his conscience and aim. The witnesses note the pray for us: "pray for us" — Gill notes for the ministers, in their arduous work; the pray for us; the the ministers; the the work; the arduous; the intercession. The expositors observe the his fellow-labourers: for him and his fellow-labourers; the his fellow-labourers; the the gospel; the the ministers; the labourers; the for us. The witnesses press the good conscience: "for we trust we have a good conscience" — a conscience clear of guile; the good conscience; the clear; the no guile; the no hypocrisy; the before God. The expositors draw the lesson on the live honestly: "in all things willing to live honestly" — sincerely desiring to walk uprightly; the live honestly; the uprightly; the sincerely; the in everything; the the aim. The witnesses observe the freely asks: conscious of his honest aim, he the more freely asks prayer; the freely asks; the his honest aim; the the more; the asks prayer; the confidence. The expositors note the trustworthy: they could pray for one whose conscience they could trust; the trustworthy; the his conscience; the his conduct; the trust; the pray. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the pray for us, the for we trust we have a good conscience in all things willing to live honestly, and the request for prayer. The verse thus records the request for prayer grounded in a good conscience — teaching that Paul asks the believers' prayers, commending himself by the integrity of his conscience and aim.
19:
"But I beseech you the rather to do this, that I may be restored to you the sooner." The witnesses note the apostle's earnest entreaty for prayer that he might return. Barnes notes it is here clearly implied that the writer was deterred from visiting them by some adverse circumstances over which he had no control, perhaps imprisonment. The witnesses note Paul beseeching them the more earnestly to pray that he might be restored to them sooner; the urgent plea for prayer toward a hoped-for reunion — that Paul presses his request for prayer with a personal earnestness: "but I beseech you the rather to do this" (to pray for him all the more) "that I may be restored to you the sooner"; the witnesses note what this reveals — Paul was being kept from them by some hindrance beyond his control (likely his imprisonment), and he so longed to be with them, and so trusted the power of their prayers, that he begged them to pray the harder, believing that their intercession might hasten his release and reunion with them; so that he counted the prayers of the saints a real means by which God might bring about his return; that I may be restored to you the sooner. The witnesses note but I beseech you the rather to do this. The witnesses note that I may be restored to you the sooner.

The witnesses note Paul's earnest plea — to pray the more that he might be restored to them sooner; counting the saints' prayers a real means by which God might hasten his return.

So the witnesses make the verse the urgent plea for prayer toward reunion. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul, kept from them by some hindrance, begs their prayers to hasten his return. The witnesses note the beseech the rather: "but I beseech you the rather to do this" — to pray for him all the more; the beseech the rather; the the more; the pray; the earnestly; the urge. The expositors observe Barnes's note: Barnes notes he was deterred by adverse circumstances, perhaps imprisonment; the Barnes; the deterred; the adverse circumstances; the imprisonment; the hindered. The witnesses press the restored sooner: "that I may be restored to you the sooner" — that he might return sooner; the restored sooner; the return; the sooner; the reunion; the to them. The expositors draw the lesson on the longed to be: he so longed to be with them; the longed to be; the with them; the desired; the reunion; the return. The witnesses observe the trusted prayer: he so trusted the power of their prayers; the trusted prayer; the the power; the their prayers; the believed; the hasten. The expositors note the a real means: he counted their prayers a real means of his return; the a real means; the their prayers; the his return; the God might; the bring about. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the but I beseech you the rather to do this, the that I may be restored to you the sooner, and the plea for prayer. The verse thus records the urgent plea for prayer toward reunion — teaching that Paul, kept from them by some hindrance, begs their prayers to hasten his return.
20:
"Now the God of peace, that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great shepherd of the sheep, through the blood of the everlasting covenant," The witnesses note the great benediction's address to the God of peace who raised Christ. Gill notes this is the concluding part of the epistle, ended with a prayer; the apostle addresses God as "the God of peace," who is so called because he is the author of peace, of reconciliation by Christ. The witnesses note Paul opening his closing prayer by addressing the God of peace who brought again from the dead the Lord Jesus, the great shepherd, through the blood of the everlasting covenant; the address to the God of peace who raised the great Shepherd by covenant blood — that Paul, as he asked their prayers, now prays for them, beginning with a rich naming of God: "the God of peace" (the author of all reconciliation and peace through Christ), "that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus" (who raised Christ from the grave, the great proof of his accepted sacrifice), "that great shepherd of the sheep" (Christ, the chief and good Shepherd who laid down his life and rose for his flock), "through the blood of the everlasting covenant"; the witnesses note that the resurrection of Christ rested on the merit of his blood, the blood that ratified the everlasting covenant of grace; so that the very ground of the believer's hope is in this risen Shepherd and his covenant blood; the God of peace, that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great shepherd of the sheep. The witnesses note the God of peace, that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus. The witnesses note that great shepherd of the sheep, through the blood of the everlasting covenant.

The witnesses note the address to the God of peace who raised the Lord Jesus, the great Shepherd, through the blood of the everlasting covenant; the believer's hope in the risen Shepherd.

So the witnesses make the verse the address to the God who raised the great Shepherd. The lesson the expositors draw is that the God of peace raised Christ, the great Shepherd, through the blood of the everlasting covenant. The witnesses note the God of peace: "the God of peace" — Gill notes the author of peace and reconciliation; the God of peace; the the author; the peace; the reconciliation; the by Christ. The expositors observe the brought from the dead: "that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus" — who raised Christ from the grave; the brought from the dead; the raised; the the grave; the the proof; the accepted. The witnesses press the great shepherd: "that great shepherd of the sheep" — Christ the chief and good Shepherd; the great shepherd; the the chief; the the good; the his flock; the laid down his life. The expositors draw the lesson on the the covenant blood: "through the blood of the everlasting covenant" — the blood that ratified the covenant; the the covenant blood; the ratified; the the covenant; the everlasting; the the blood. The witnesses observe the resurrection on the blood: the resurrection rested on the merit of his blood; the resurrection on the blood; the the merit; the his blood; the rested; the raised. The expositors note the the ground of hope: the believer's hope is in the risen Shepherd and his blood; the the ground of hope; the the risen Shepherd; the his blood; the hope. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that great shepherd of the sheep through the blood of the everlasting covenant, and the God who raised the Shepherd. The verse thus records the address to the God who raised the great Shepherd — teaching that the God of peace raised Christ through the blood of the everlasting covenant.
21:
"Make you perfect in every good work to do his will, working in you that which is wellpleasing in his sight, through Jesus Christ; to whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen." The witnesses note the prayer that God perfect them for his will and work in them what pleases him. Gill notes a good work is what is done in obedience to the command of God, in faith, from a principle of love, in the name and strength of Christ, and with a view to the glory of God. The witnesses note Paul praying that God make them perfect in every good work to do his will, working in them what is well-pleasing through Jesus Christ; the petition that God himself fit and enable his people for every good work — that Paul names the great request of his benediction: that this God of peace would "make you perfect in every good work to do his will" (fully fit, equip, and furnish them for all obedience) — and the secret of it, "working in you that which is wellpleasing in his sight" (God himself working within them the very obedience he requires) "through Jesus Christ"; the witnesses note the precious truth here — that the good works God requires he himself works in his people, so that all our doing of his will is the fruit of his own gracious operation within us through Christ; and Paul closes with a doxology, "to whom be glory for ever and ever. Amen"; working in you that which is wellpleasing in his sight, through Jesus Christ. The witnesses note make you perfect in every good work to do his will. The witnesses note working in you that which is wellpleasing in his sight, through Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note the prayer that God perfect them for every good work — working in them what pleases him; the good works God requires he himself works in his people through Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the prayer that God fit and work in his people. The lesson the expositors draw is that God himself perfects and works in his people the obedience he requires, through Christ. The witnesses note the make you perfect: "make you perfect in every good work to do his will" — Gill notes fully fit and equip for obedience; the make you perfect; the fit; the equip; the every good work; the his will. The expositors observe the a good work: a good work is done in faith, love, and Christ's strength; the a good work; the in faith; the love; the Christ's strength; the obedience. The witnesses press the working in you: "working in you that which is wellpleasing in his sight" — God works within them the obedience; the working in you; the within them; the the obedience; the well-pleasing; the God works. The expositors draw the lesson on the through Christ: "through Jesus Christ" — all through Christ; the through Christ; the all; the the secret; the his operation; the Christ. The witnesses observe the his own work: the good works God requires he himself works; the his own work; the God works; the the good works; the requires; the his operation. The expositors note the doxology: Paul closes with a doxology, "to whom be glory for ever"; the doxology; the glory; the for ever; the Amen; the praise. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the make you perfect in every good work to do his will, the working in you that which is wellpleasing in his sight through Jesus Christ, and the prayer that God work. The verse thus records the prayer that God fit and work in his people — teaching that God himself perfects and works in his people the obedience he requires, through Christ.
22:
"And I beseech you, brethren, suffer the word of exhortation: for I have written a letter unto you in few words." The witnesses note the apostle's tender entreaty that they bear with his exhortation. Gill notes the apostle closes his epistle in a tender and affectionate manner, calling them "brethren," and speaking not in an authoritative way nor by way of advice, but by entreaty. The witnesses note Paul beseeching the brethren to bear with the word of exhortation, since he had written to them in few words; the gentle plea to receive the letter's exhortation in good part — that Paul, drawing to a close, makes a humble request: "I beseech you, brethren, suffer the word of exhortation" (bear with, receive patiently and kindly, the exhortation and admonition of this whole letter) — and he softens it with a gracious reason: "for I have written a letter unto you in few words" (the letter is, considering its great themes, comparatively brief); the witnesses note the apostle's tenderness — though he had spoken weighty and searching things, he asks not as a commander but as a brother that they take his words in good part, all the more readily because he had been so brief; so that the spirit of true exhortation is humble and affectionate; suffer the word of exhortation. The witnesses note suffer the word of exhortation. The witnesses note for I have written a letter unto you in few words.

The witnesses note Paul's tender plea — to bear with the word of exhortation, since he wrote in few words; the apostle asking not as a commander but as a brother that they take his words in good part.

So the witnesses make the verse the gentle plea to receive the exhortation. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul humbly asks the brethren to bear with the letter's exhortation, all the more as it was brief. The witnesses note the beseech: "I beseech you, brethren, suffer the word of exhortation" — Gill notes he speaks by entreaty, calling them brethren; the beseech; the brethren; the entreaty; the not authoritative; the tender. The expositors observe the suffer: "suffer the word of exhortation" — bear with the admonition kindly; the suffer; the bear with; the receive; the the admonition; the patiently. The witnesses press the in few words: "for I have written a letter unto you in few words" — the letter is comparatively brief; the in few words; the brief; the comparatively; the the themes; the short. The expositors draw the lesson on the gracious reason: the brevity is a gracious reason to bear with it; the gracious reason; the the brevity; the bear with; the the more; the readily. The witnesses observe the not a commander: he asks not as a commander but as a brother; the not a commander; the a brother; the asks; the in good part; the tender. The expositors note the humble exhortation: the spirit of true exhortation is humble and affectionate; the humble exhortation; the affectionate; the humble; the the spirit; the tender. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the suffer the word of exhortation, the for I have written a letter unto you in few words, and the gentle plea. The verse thus records the gentle plea to receive the exhortation — teaching that Paul humbly asks the brethren to bear with the letter's exhortation, all the more as it was brief.
23:
"Know ye that our brother Timothy is set at liberty; with whom, if he come shortly, I will see you." The witnesses note the news of Timothy's release and the hoped-for visit. Gill notes this is the same Timothy to whom the Apostle Paul wrote two epistles, often calling him his son, though sometimes his brother as here; he was set at liberty, or "dismissed." The witnesses note Paul telling them that brother Timothy is set at liberty, with whom, if he comes shortly, he will see them; the personal news of Timothy's freedom and the prospect of a joint visit — that Paul shares a piece of glad news and a hope: "know ye that our brother Timothy is set at liberty" (Timothy, his beloved fellow-worker, has been released, having himself been imprisoned for the gospel) — and Paul looks forward to coming with him: "with whom, if he come shortly, I will see you"; the witnesses note the warm fellowship of the gospel ministers — Paul rejoices in Timothy's freedom and plans, should Timothy come soon, to visit the Hebrews together with him, so that personal love and the bonds of ministry run through even the close of this great doctrinal letter; know ye that our brother Timothy is set at liberty. The witnesses note know ye that our brother Timothy is set at liberty. The witnesses note with whom, if he come shortly, I will see you.

The witnesses note the news of Timothy's release and the hoped-for joint visit — the warm fellowship of gospel ministers running through even the close of this great doctrinal letter.

So the witnesses make the verse the news of Timothy's freedom and a hoped visit. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul shares the glad news of Timothy's release and the hope of visiting together. The witnesses note the Timothy: "our brother Timothy" — Gill notes Paul's beloved fellow-worker; the Timothy; the brother; the fellow-worker; the beloved; the his son. The expositors observe the set at liberty: "is set at liberty" — released from imprisonment; the set at liberty; the released; the dismissed; the imprisoned; the freed. The witnesses press the if he come shortly: "with whom, if he come shortly" — should Timothy come soon; the if he come shortly; the soon; the Timothy; the come; the the prospect. The expositors draw the lesson on the I will see you: "I will see you" — Paul plans to visit with him; the I will see you; the visit; the with him; the the prospect; the see. The witnesses observe the warm fellowship: the warm fellowship of gospel ministers; the warm fellowship; the the ministers; the the bonds; the love; the the gospel. The expositors note the through the close: personal love runs through even the close of the letter; the through the close; the personal love; the the bonds; the the letter; the warm. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the know ye that our brother Timothy is set at liberty, the with whom if he come shortly I will see you, and the news of Timothy. The verse thus records the news of Timothy's freedom and a hoped visit — teaching that Paul shares the glad news of Timothy's release and the hope of visiting together.
24:
"Salute all them that have the rule over you, and all the saints. They of Italy salute you." The witnesses note the closing salutations to leaders and saints. Gill notes "salute all them that have the rule over you" are the pastors and officers of the church of the Hebrews, and "all the saints" the several members of the church, set apart to holiness by God, sanctified by Christ. The witnesses note Paul sending greetings to all their rulers and all the saints, with the salutation of those of Italy; the exchange of Christian greetings closing the letter — that Paul ends with the warm courtesy of Christian love: "salute all them that have the rule over you" (greet the pastors and leaders of the church on his behalf) "and all the saints" (and every member of the holy company) — and he conveys greetings from the believers where he was: "they of Italy salute you" (the Christians of Italy send their love to the Hebrews); the witnesses note this gracious exchange of greetings between churches and saints far apart, the bond of love in Christ uniting believers across distance; salute all them that have the rule over you, and all the saints. The witnesses note salute all them that have the rule over you, and all the saints. The witnesses note they of Italy salute you.

The witnesses note the closing salutations — greetings to all the leaders and saints, and the love of those of Italy; the bond of love in Christ uniting believers across distance.

So the witnesses make the verse the closing exchange of Christian greetings. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul greets the leaders and all the saints, conveying the love of the Italian believers. The witnesses note the salute the rulers: "salute all them that have the rule over you" — Gill notes the pastors and officers; the salute the rulers; the the pastors; the the officers; the the leaders; the greet. The expositors observe the all the saints: "and all the saints" — every member of the holy company; the all the saints; the every member; the the holy company; the sanctified; the the church. The witnesses press the on his behalf: greet them on Paul's behalf; the on his behalf; the greet; the for Paul; the salute; the convey. The expositors draw the lesson on the of Italy: "they of Italy salute you" — the Italian Christians send their love; the of Italy; the the Italian Christians; the send their love; the salute; the greetings. The witnesses observe the across distance: the bond of love uniting believers across distance; the across distance; the the bond of love; the believers; the united; the in Christ. The expositors note the gracious exchange: a gracious exchange of greetings between churches; the gracious exchange; the greetings; the the churches; the the saints; the love. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the salute all them that have the rule over you and all the saints, the they of Italy salute you, and the closing salutations. The verse thus records the closing exchange of Christian greetings — teaching that Paul greets the leaders and all the saints, conveying the love of the Italian believers.
25:
"Grace be with you all. Amen." The witnesses note the closing benediction of grace. Gill notes this is the apostle's usual salutation in all his epistles, in which he wishes renewed discoveries of the love and favour of God, fresh communications of grace from Christ, an abundant supply of the spirit of grace, and a continuance of the gospel. The witnesses note Paul closing the epistle with the benediction, "Grace be with you all. Amen"; the final apostolic blessing of grace on all the readers — that Paul, as in all his letters, seals this great epistle with his accustomed and beloved benediction: "Grace be with you all" — the invoking upon every reader, without exception ("you all"), of the free favour and grace of God in Christ, with all its fruits and blessings — and he sets the solemn "Amen" upon it; the witnesses note the fitness of this close — a letter so full of grace, of Christ, and of the better covenant, ends, as it should, with the prayer that grace itself might rest and abide upon all the people of God; so that the last word of Hebrews is grace; Grace be with you all. Amen. The witnesses note Grace be with you all. The witnesses note Amen.

The witnesses note the final benediction of grace — "Grace be with you all. Amen"; a letter so full of grace fittingly ending with the prayer that grace itself rest upon all God's people.

So the witnesses make the verse the closing benediction of grace. The lesson the expositors draw is that Paul seals the epistle, as ever, with the invoking of God's grace upon all the readers. The witnesses note the grace: "Grace be with you all" — Gill notes Paul's usual closing salutation; the grace; the usual; the closing; the salutation; the benediction. The expositors observe the favour and grace: invoking the free favour and grace of God in Christ; the favour and grace; the free; the of God; the in Christ; the the fruits. The witnesses press the you all: "you all" — upon every reader without exception; the you all; the every reader; the without exception; the all; the upon all. The expositors draw the lesson on the the Amen: "Amen" — the solemn seal upon it; the the Amen; the the seal; the solemn; the so be it; the close. The witnesses observe the fitness: the fitness of this close to a letter full of grace; the fitness; the the close; the full of grace; the Christ; the the covenant. The expositors note the last word grace: the last word of Hebrews is grace; the last word grace; the Hebrews; the grace; the the close; the ends. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Grace be with you all, the Amen, and the closing benediction. The verse thus records the closing benediction of grace — teaching that Paul seals the epistle, as ever, with the invoking of God's grace upon all the readers.
